title: Add Unit Economics pipeline Grafana dashboards + freshness/completeness alerts
preview:
## Summary
Adds internal Grafana dashboards and alert rules for the Unit Economics pipeline (hourly aggregation + warehouse views) so we can detect missing hours, freshness lag, and dimension coverage regressions before Finance/Product consume the Console dashboard.

This PR lives in `observability-pack` and only ships dashboards/alerts (no runtime or pipeline code changes). It assumes the aggregator and DQ checks emit the metrics introduced in the Redwood monorepo work (ENG-8426/ENG-8429).

## Motivation
During dogfood we repeatedly saw "looks like a cost spike" that was actually missing output tokens or delayed aggregation. The Console page is not the right place to diagnose pipeline health, and SRE asked for a standard set of panels + alerts aligned to the proposed SLO.

## What’s included
- Grafana dashboard: **Unit Economics — Pipeline Health**
  - Freshness lag (now - last successful hourly partition) by environment
  - Ingestion completeness (% expected hourly partitions present in last 24h)
  - Aggregation job runtime and failure rate
  - Warehouse view query latency (p95/p99) for the dashboard endpoints
  - Backfill activity (rows written/hour + throttling indicators)
- Grafana dashboard: **Unit Economics — Coverage & Drift**
  - Output token totals by hour with 7d baseline band (detect sudden drops)
  - Dimension coverage panels:
    - % rows missing `region`
    - % rows missing `customer_tier`
    - % rows missing `model_id` / `model_version`
    - `quantization_profile` cardinality + unknown/other bucket rate
  - Top offenders tables (regions with high null mapping, tiers with zero coverage)
- Alert rules (Prometheus-style via Jsonnet)
  - `unit_econ_pipeline_freshness_lag_hours > 2` (page)
  - `unit_econ_pipeline_missing_partitions_24h > 1` (page)
  - `unit_econ_output_tokens_drop_ratio < 0.6` vs trailing 7d median (page; 2 consecutive evals)
  - `unit_econ_dimension_null_rate{dim=region} > 0.5%` (ticket)
  - `unit_econ_dimension_null_rate{dim=customer_tier} > 0.5%` (ticket)
  - `unit_econ_quant_profile_unknown_rate > 0.2%` (ticket)
  - `unit_econ_warehouse_view_latency_p99_ms > 2500` (ticket)
- Alert routing + runbook links
  - Pages route to `#eng-sre` and `#eng-platform` with oncall mention
  - Tickets route to `#eng-platform` with a lower-noise policy
  - Runbook URL is included in annotations and points to the unit economics oncall guide (ENG-8435)

## Notes on thresholds / noise
- Freshness/page thresholds mirror the proposed SLO: "hourly data available within 90 minutes" with a buffer for warehouse maintenance windows.
- Token-drop alert uses a ratio vs trailing 7d median per environment, because absolute tokens vary substantially by weekday and launches.
- Dimension null-rate thresholds are set to 0.5% for now to catch mapping regressions without paging on small blips. We can tighten after 2 weeks of baseline.

## Files / areas changed (high level)
- `dashboards/unit-economics/pipeline-health.jsonnet` (new)
- `dashboards/unit-economics/coverage-and-drift.jsonnet` (new)
- `alerts/unit-economics/pipeline-alerts.jsonnet` (new)
- `alerts/unit-economics/README.md` (new; runbook + alert intent)
- `lib/panels/unit-economics.libsonnet` (new; shared panels + variable definitions)
- `team-ownership/oncall-rotations.libsonnet` (updated routing for econ pipeline)

## Example rule (illustrative)
The freshness alert is keyed off the "last successful hourly partition" metric exported by the aggregator job:
- expr: `time() - unit_econ_agg_last_success_timestamp_seconds > 2 * 3600`
- for: `10m`
- severity: page
- annotations include runbook and a "first checks" list.

## Testing / verification
- Rendered dashboards locally with `make grafana-render` and verified variable wiring (env/cluster/warehouse).
- Loaded into staging Grafana and confirmed panels populate for the last 24h.
- Dry-ran alerts in staging with lowered thresholds to confirm routing + annotations.

## Rollout plan
1) Merge and deploy observability-pack.
2) Enable alerts in staging for 24h.
3) Enable production alerts with `ticket` severities only for 48h (collect baseline).
4) Promote freshness + missing partitions + token-drop to `page`.

## Open questions
- Do we want a separate Finance-friendly "health badge" summary panel embedded in the Console dashboard? (out of scope here)
- Should `unit_econ_warehouse_view_latency_*` alerts be owned by Console oncall instead of Platform? Current routing goes to Platform due to materialization/partitioning dependencies.

## PR conversation highlights
- Allison Grant: Requested that paging alerts include direct runbook links and a short decision tree. Updated annotations to include: verify ingestion lag, check latest partitions table, run backfill if needed.
- Sean Gallagher: Asked to avoid paging on single-hour drops during scheduled warehouse maintenance. Added `for: 10m` and excluded known maintenance window label where present.
- Caleb Johnson: Flagged that `quantization_profile` could become high-cardinality; requested a dedicated panel + alert on unknown/other bucket rate rather than raw cardinality alone. Added both.
- Mei Lin: Noted the p99 latency threshold should be environment-specific. Added separate thresholds: staging 4000ms ticket, prod 2500ms ticket.

## CI
- lint/jsonnet: pass
- dashboards render: pass
- unit tests (alerts schema validation): pass

Merged via squash.

Refs: ENG-8429 (data quality checks/anomaly alerts), ENG-8435 (oncall runbook/playbook docs).

title: Add canary check for output-token metering parity (streaming + non-streaming)
preview:
Context: we’ve had at least one production regression where streaming responses under-counted output tokens in metering, which then cascaded into Unit Economics dashboards (blended $/1M output tokens) and reconciliation views. This PR adds an automated canary that detects output-token metering regressions before a gateway/runtime change is widely rolled out.

What this adds:
- New canary suite `metering_output_token_parity` that runs against a small allowlisted canary tenant in prod.
- Exercises BOTH streaming and non-streaming generation paths (including tool-call shaped outputs) and validates:
  1) output_tokens reported by the API response metadata
  2) output_tokens recorded by metering events (usage pipeline)
  3) that the delta between (1) and (2) is within a configured tolerance after accounting for ingestion latency
- Adds guardrails for ingestion lag: the canary polls the metering lookup API for up to 6 minutes (configurable) before declaring missing events.
- Emits a single structured result event per run (pass/fail + diagnostics) and wires alert routing:
  - warning to #eng-platform when parity drift > 0.5% for a single run
  - page oncall (telemetry) when drift > 1.0% for 3 consecutive runs OR when missing metering events exceed 2% of requests

Key implementation details:
- Request set: 18 requests per run (9 streaming, 9 non-streaming) with fixed prompt fixtures to keep variance low.
- Models: uses `rw-llama-3.1-8b-instruct` (cheap + stable) pinned to a specific model version for determinism.
- Tokenization alignment: uses the server-side token counts only (we do NOT attempt client-side tokenization); the canary is specifically checking parity between two server-side sources of truth.
- Correlation: uses `x-request-id` and `metering_correlation_id` to look up metering records; falls back to time window + route + model + canary tenant ID when correlation ID missing.
- Flake reduction: retries on 429/503 with exponential backoff; run is considered inconclusive (non-paging) if infra is degraded and error rate > 30%.

Rollout:
- Enabled in dry-run mode for 24h (records metrics but does not page).
- After 24h, flip `CANARY_METERING_PARITY_PAGING_ENABLED=true` in the perf-canary prod deployment.

Notes / limitations:
- This is not a billing reconciliation test. It only checks output token counts parity between API-side accounting and metering events.
- Known source of noise: very rare duplicate metering events; the canary de-dupes by (correlation_id, sequence).

Operational links:
- Runbook draft: https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/x/unit-econ-output-token-parity (will be finalized alongside ENG-8435)

Why now:
- The internal Unit Economics dashboard (v1) depends on reliable output token counts (especially streaming); this canary is intended to catch regressions like the one fixed in `redwood` PR-19355 before they impact exec/finance reporting.

title: Add data quality checks + anomaly alerts for unit economics pipeline
preview:
Context: We’ve seen multiple cases where the Unit Economics dashboard showed sudden improvements or drops that were actually pipeline issues (most commonly ingestion lag or missing dimensions), e.g. the “hours missing” incident thread and earlier streaming token undercount investigations. This PR adds first-pass data quality checks and anomaly alerts for the hourly unit economics aggregation outputs so we can detect regressions quickly and avoid Finance/Product interpreting bad data.

What this PR does: (1) Completeness checks per hour: verifies presence of expected rows for core dimensions (region, customer_tier, model) and flags coverage below threshold; (2) Missing dimension checks: alerts when unknown/NULL region or customer_tier exceeds a configured percentage; (3) Token-volume anomaly checks: compares output_tokens to a trailing baseline (7d median by {region,tier} and global) and alerts on sudden drops/spikes; (4) Freshness checks: alerts if the latest successfully published hour is older than N minutes; (5) Emits standardized alert events to PagerDuty + Slack via our existing “observability-pack” sink, with runbook links and dashboards.

Notes/constraints: - These checks operate on the post-aggregation tables (warehouse) rather than raw metering to keep signal stable and avoid cardinality explosions. - Baselines are computed on “finance_view” (rate-card-stable) by default; “live_view” is available but not used for alerting to avoid false positives during backfills. - Thresholds are intentionally conservative in v1; tuning will happen after a week of dogfooding.

Testing: - Added unit tests for threshold logic and baseline computation edge cases (new region/tier appearing, backfill window overlaps). - Ran ad-hoc validation in staging by simulating missing tiers and forcing a token drop.

Rollout plan: Merge behind ECON_DQ_ALERTS_ENABLED flag default OFF in prod. SRE (Sean) will enable for internal dogfood after we observe 48h of clean staging behavior. Finance will be notified once alert noise is acceptable.

Follow-ups: - Add per-model-version anomaly checks once model_id normalization is fully rolled out everywhere. - Add explicit detection for streaming/non-streaming parity drift once the streaming metering canary is fully green for a full week.

Refs: ENG-8429, ENG-8430; incident context: “metric drop / hours missing” + internal backfill performance issues; runbook draft will land separately (ENG-8435).

title: Add internal feature flag + RBAC gating for Unit Economics dashboard
preview: Context: The new internal Unit Economics dashboard surfaces sensitive cost data (blended $/1M output tokens, drilldowns by model/region/tier). We need to ensure access is restricted to a small internal allowlist initially and that all access is auditable for Finance and Security sign-off. This PR adds (1) a server-side feature flag gate and (2) RBAC policy updates + enforcement in the Console backend so that the Unit Economics endpoints and UI route are not reachable unless explicitly granted. Changes: - Feature flag: add `console.internal_unit_economics_dashboard` (server-side, env-scoped). Default OFF in all environments; can be enabled in prod only for the internal allowlist group. - RBAC: introduce permission `console:unit_economics:read` and require it for all unit economics API endpoints + the console route loader. - Enforcement points: apply gating at the HTTP router layer and again at the service layer (defense-in-depth) so future endpoints don’t accidentally bypass checks. - Audit logging: emit an audit event on successful access and on denied access (includes actor, org, reason: missing_flag vs missing_permission). - Admin tooling: add a small helper to map the initial allowlist group to the permission for dogfood rollout. Notes: - The UI still renders behind the flag, but if the backend gate denies access, the page shows the standard “Permission denied” state (UI work in PR-19378). - This intentionally does not add any customer-level permissions; v1 is internal-only (no customer exposure). Testing: unit tests cover flag off -> 404/403 behavior, permission missing -> 403, both enabled -> success, and audit event emission. Follow-ups: - Once Finance signs off on metric definitions, we can expand the allowlist and/or move to role-based groups (Finance, Platform Eng, SRE) instead of named individuals. - Consider adding a “redacted mode” for broader internal visibility if needed (out of v1 scope).

title: Backfill tooling for 2025Q1 unit economics aggregates (idempotent + throttled)
preview: Context: Finance needs QTD and quarter-close reporting in the internal Unit Economics dashboard, but the hourly aggregation job only covered data from the initial cutover onward. This PR adds an idempotent backfill tool to populate historical aggregates for 2025Q1 (and generally any arbitrary time window) while protecting the warehouse and avoiding double-counting. Scope: (1) Adds a backfill CLI (unit-econ-backfill) that replays the same aggregation logic as the hourly job over a time range; (2) Implements idempotency via per-hour partition overwrite semantics and a backfill_runs audit table; (3) Adds throttling (max concurrent partitions, adaptive sleep on warehouse pressure) and a maintenance-window mode; (4) Adds verification queries and a reconciliation helper view to compare pre/post backfill totals; (5) Updates runbook docs referenced by alerts. Idempotency model: the backfill operates on canonical hourly partitions (dt_hour UTC) in the materialized aggregate tables. For each dt_hour, it writes to a staging table and then atomically swaps/overwrites the target partition. A backfill_runs table records (run_id, requested_range, effective_partitions, rate_card_version, git_sha, started_at, completed_at, status) for auditability and for detecting partial runs. Concurrency + safety: default max_concurrency=4, with a --warehouse-safe flag that drops to 2 and increases sleep on query timeouts; --dry-run prints partition plan and estimated work; --resume uses backfill_runs to skip completed partitions; --force allows reprocessing (for rate card updates or logic changes) but still overwrites per-hour partitions to avoid duplication. Notes: We explicitly do not backfill raw metering events; we backfill derived aggregates only. This is intended for internal reporting and reconciliation and is gated behind internal authz. Testing: Added unit tests for partition planning/idempotency and integration tests against a local warehouse test container with seeded metering + rate card fixtures. Rollout: Run once for 2025-01-01..2025-03-31 (inclusive) in the analytics maintenance window; SRE will monitor warehouse saturation dashboards; Finance will validate QTD totals using the reconciliation view (linked below). Follow-ups: If Finance requests prior quarters, we can reuse the same tool; we may want a weekly scheduled backfill for late-arriving metering data, but that is out of scope for v1.

title: Unit economics: add rate limits + response caching for dashboard query APIs
preview: Summary: Adds server-side caching and request rate limiting to the internal Unit Economics dashboard query APIs introduced in PR #19374. This is to (1) protect the warehouse/materialized views from repeated identical queries (especially top-N + timeseries defaults), (2) reduce load during Finance/exec review windows, and (3) improve dashboard responsiveness for common filters. Context: During dogfood we saw heavy burst traffic from the Console UI due to filter changes + auto-refresh + multiple panels firing in parallel. In the worst case, a single user interaction triggered 6-10 warehouse queries with identical group_by/filter payloads (only differing by panel). This PR implements deterministic cache keys and a small set of safe defaults that do not change correctness semantics (cached responses are bounded by short TTLs and include freshness metadata). Design notes: 1) Two cache profiles: "live" and "finance". Live is the default used by the dashboard UI (short TTL). Finance is intended for FP&A/QTD views where stability is preferred over second-to-second freshness (longer TTL), and is only enabled for specific endpoints/queries used in the KPI pack. 2) Cache keys are derived from the normalized query payload (filters + group_by + time_bucket + metric + sort + pagination) plus the caller's access scope (RBAC role + allowlisted feature flag). We explicitly do NOT include user_id in the key so a popular view can be reused across users, but we do include authz scope to avoid leaking data across roles. 3) Rate limiting is applied per user (preferred) or per IP (fallback), with separate buckets for (a) time series endpoints and (b) breakdown/top-N endpoints. We chose conservative defaults and added metrics/alerts to tune. 4) Adds response headers: X-Redwood-Cache (HIT/MISS/BYPASS), X-Redwood-Cache-TTL-Seconds, and X-Redwood-Data-As-Of (UTC timestamp) so UI and Finance can reason about freshness. Implementation details: - New middleware: economicsQueryGuard() which enforces (a) RBAC check (existing), (b) rate limit, (c) optional caching. - Caching uses our shared CacheService abstraction with Redis backend (same cluster used by Console), namespaced under unit_econ:query:v1. We cap cached payload size to 1.5MB and skip caching when the response exceeds the cap. - TTLs: live = 60s for time series and 120s for breakdown/top-N; finance = 15m for time series and 30m for breakdown/top-N. TTLs are configurable via flags: UNIT_ECON_CACHE_TTL_LIVE_SECONDS, UNIT_ECON_CACHE_TTL_FINANCE_SECONDS. - Cache bypass: request header X-Redwood-Bypass-Cache: true (allowlisted to internal admin role only) and query param bypass_cache=true (admin only). Bypass requests still write-through the cache unless bypass_write=true (admin only; intended for debugging). - Rate limits (initial): time series = 60 req/min/user, breakdown = 30 req/min/user, with burst=2x. Per-IP fallback is 30 req/min/ip for time series and 15 req/min/ip for breakdown. - Adds metrics: unit_econ_api_cache_hit_total{endpoint,profile}, unit_econ_api_cache_miss_total{endpoint,profile}, unit_econ_api_rl_block_total{endpoint,reason}, unit_econ_api_query_duration_ms{endpoint,warehouse=true/false}. - Adds structured logs for cache key + normalization hash (not the raw filters) to help debug "why didn't I hit cache" without leaking customer IDs. Security/privacy: The cache key derivation explicitly redacts/normalizes high-cardinality inputs and never logs raw customer identifiers. Responses remain aggregated; no per-customer breakdown is introduced here. The cache namespace is internal-only and protected by service credentials; however, we additionally include caller scope in the key to prevent any future role-based divergence from causing cross-scope reuse. Testing: - Unit tests for query normalization/canonicalization (ordering, default values, null vs omitted). - Integration tests for caching behavior: identical queries HIT, modified filter MISS, scope change MISS. - Integration tests for rate limiting (429) with deterministic clock. - Load test script (internal) for the default dashboard view shows ~72% cache hit after warmup; warehouse query count reduced ~4.6x for the same UI interaction pattern. Rollout plan: - Deploy behind feature flag UNIT_ECON_QUERY_GUARDS_ENABLED (default off). - Enable in staging for 24h, verify cache hit rate + 429 counts + no increase in error rate. - Gradually enable in prod for internal users (10% -> 50% -> 100%) coordinated with SRE (Sean) and Observability (Allison). - If Finance reports stale reads, switch Finance profile queries to bypass cache temporarily while we tune TTL or panel refresh behavior. title: Add runbook links + remediation hints to unit economics alert payloads
preview:
Context

We’ve had a couple of "unknown page / where do I start" escalations when the Unit Economics pipeline alerts fire (freshness/completeness/anomaly checks). The alerts today include the alert name, labels, and a few raw counters, but they don’t provide:
- a direct runbook link
- the most common remediation steps
- a pointer to validation queries / backfill command entrypoints

This PR updates the alert payloads emitted by the economics pipeline (and the alert templates used by the internal notifier) to include runbook links + short remediation hints so first responders can take action without context switching.

What’s in this PR

1) Alert payload schema enhancements
- Adds optional fields to the canonical alert payload:
  - runbook_url (string)
  - remediation (short bullet-like string; kept intentionally brief)
  - dashboard_url (string; used when we have a Grafana panel / Console deep link)
- Keeps backward compatibility: existing consumers ignore unknown fields; no required field changes.

2) Runbook mapping + link hygiene
- Introduces a small mapping table keyed by alert_id (NOT by free-form alert name) to avoid drift as names/labels evolve.
- Uses stable internal go-links for Confluence/Grafana targets where possible (prevents Slack history from rotting when pages move).
- Adds a default fallback for unknown alerts: runbook_url points to the index page for the Unit Economics oncall guide.

3) Alert renderer / Slack + PagerDuty payloads
- Slack: appends:
  - "Runbook: <link>"
  - "First steps: …"
  - "Dashboards: <link>" (when present)
- PagerDuty: includes the same fields in custom_details so the oncaller sees it in the incident UI.

4) Tests
- Unit tests cover:
  - mapping lookup behavior
  - fallback behavior
  - rendering of Slack blocks + PD custom_details
  - ensures remediation stays < 280 chars (avoids noisy payloads / truncation)

Examples

Before (Slack)
- "unit-econ.hourly_aggregation.missing_hours" + labels

After (Slack)
- unit-econ.hourly_aggregation.missing_hours
- Runbook: https://go.redwood/runbook-unit-econ-missing-hours
- First steps: check ingestion lag; validate hourly completeness view; if lagged, re-run aggregation for affected hours; if dropped rows, check region mapping + null handling
- Dashboards: https://go.redwood/grafana-unit-econ-pipeline

Rollout / risk

- This is safe to roll out broadly: schema change is additive.
- Scoped initially to Unit Economics alerts only.
- If we discover any formatting regressions, we can disable via the notifier config flag `include_runbook_links` (defaults ON for unit-econ routes).

References
- ENG-8435 (Create runbook pages and link from alerts)
- ENG-8429 (Completeness/anomaly checks)
- Related: pr-19383-add-data-quality-checks-for-economics-pipeline.json (introduces the checks that fire these alerts)

Screenshots
- Included in internal review (Slack block formatting) — see "Checks" artifacts in CI run.

title: Bugfix: preserve rows when region mapping is null/unknown in unit economics aggregation
preview:
Context: After merging the region normalization work, we noticed intermittent drops in hourly unit economics aggregates where a small (but non-trivial) slice of metering rows had region unset or had a value that didn’t map (e.g. legacy colo labels, nulls from older gateway paths, or "unknown" emitted during partial outages). The aggregation job used an INNER JOIN to the canonical region mapping table, which caused those rows to be dropped entirely. This under-counted output_tokens and made $/1M output tokens look artificially worse/better depending on which spend inputs were present for that hour.

What this PR does: Update the unit economics aggregation query and the region mapping helper to be null-safe and non-dropping: (1) switch the join to LEFT JOIN, (2) COALESCE to a canonical region value of "unknown" when mapping fails, and (3) emit an explicit counter for unmapped rows so we can alert on drift instead of silently losing data. Also adds a small regression test covering null + unmapped region values.

Why this is safe: The change only affects how we assign the region dimension for rows that previously disappeared; mapped rows are unchanged. Downstream views already treat region as a dimension with a bounded value set, and adding "unknown" is within our cardinality guardrails.

Test plan: Run unit tests for the query builder + add a warehouse integration test fixture that includes null and unmapped region inputs; validate that output_tokens totals match pre-join totals and that the "unknown" bucket contains the expected rows.

Rollout: Deploy with the next scheduled analytics job release; monitor (a) unit_econ.region_mapping.unmapped_rows, (b) hourly completeness checks, and (c) reconciliation deltas vs billing totals. If the unmapped counter spikes, we’ll investigate upstream emitters rather than letting the aggregator hide the issue.

title: Backpressure controls + max lag safety (auto-pause) for downstream outages
preview:
Context: We had a beta incident where Splunk HEC throttling (429 + intermittent 503) caused exporter lag to spike and sustained retries increased queue pressure and CPU, impacting other tenants sharing the same exporter shard (see #incidents thread 1739559010 and SUP-18531 follow-up). This PR introduces explicit backpressure controls and a max-lag safety mechanism to keep the system stable during downstream outages while preserving at-least-once semantics.

What’s in this PR: (1) Max lag threshold per destination with auto-pause: if export_lag_seconds for a (tenant, destination) exceeds configurable max_lag_seconds for a sustained window, we stop pulling new batches for that destination and mark it PAUSED (reason=MAX_LAG) while continuing to process other destinations/tenants. (2) Backpressure based on in-flight bytes and retry backlog: introduce per-destination in_flight_bytes limits and a bounded retry queue; when exceeded we apply pull-side backoff rather than pushing more work into retries. (3) Circuit breaker integration for adapters: adapter-specific throttling signals (e.g., Splunk 429, Datadog 429/5xx) now feed a shared circuit breaker that increases backoff, and can trip into PAUSED when combined with lag growth. (4) Operator controls: new CLI commands to unpause a destination and to override max lag for controlled recovery. (5) Observability: new Prometheus metrics and structured log fields for pause/unpause transitions, reasons, and “time paused”; status endpoint now includes pause state and last_pause_reason.

Delivery semantics: No events are dropped. Auto-pause stops the exporter from advancing its checkpoint for the paused destination, so events remain available for replay/backfill once the downstream recovers and the destination is unpaused. This is consistent with our at-least-once contract; downstream dedup remains based on event_id.

Configuration: Adds exporter config keys (with safe defaults): max_lag_seconds (default 21600 / 6h), max_lag_grace_period_seconds (default 300), max_in_flight_bytes (default 64MiB), max_retry_queue_batches (default 500), pause_cooldown_seconds (default 900). All are feature-flagged behind BACKPRESSURE_V1 to limit beta blast radius.

Testing: unit tests for PauseController transitions; integration test with Splunk stub that returns sustained 429 and verifies: lag rises -> pause triggers -> pulling stops -> unpause resumes and drains; chaos-ish test ensures healthy destination continues despite one paused. Load test notes included in commit message, based on SRE backpressure experiments doc.

Rollout plan: Enable BACKPRESSURE_V1 for beta tenants only after deploying exporter + dashboards. Default max lag is conservative; SRE can tune per-tenant via config overrides if needed.

Checklist: [x] Metrics + dashboards hooks (exporter_paused, pause_reason, pause_duration_seconds) [x] Runbook updates TODO in separate PR (Sean requested) [x] Security review: no new PII; logs redact destination secrets [x] Feature flag gated (BACKPRESSURE_V1) [x] Integration tests cover 429/5xx.

Follow-ups (not in this PR): (a) surface pause/unpause controls in Console (control plane) (b) alerting thresholds tuning once we have 1-2 weeks of beta data (c) doc updates for customer-facing explanation of auto-pause + recovery expectations.

title: Bootstrap audit exporter service skeleton + CI + local dev harness
preview:
## Summary
Bootstraps the new `audit-log-exporter` service repository with a minimal runnable skeleton, CI workflows, a base configuration model, and a local dev harness to support early iteration.

This is the first landing step for ENG-5139. No adapters, no delivery semantics, and no production wiring in this PR; those will follow in subsequent PRs.

## Motivation / Context
We need a dedicated service to read audit events (initially via stubbed source in local dev), apply tenant scoping, and deliver to downstream destinations with retries/checkpointing. Before we can iterate on architecture and adapters, we need:
- Standard project layout + build tooling
- Baseline CI (lint/test/build, container build)
- A config model (typed) that can evolve with control-plane config sync
- A local harness so devs can run the service end-to-end without needing the full control plane

## What’s included
- Service scaffold (Go)
  - `cmd/audit-exporter` main entrypoint
  - `internal/server` HTTP server with `/healthz` + `/readyz`
  - `internal/config` base config model + validation
  - `internal/logging` structured logging setup (json)
- CI / tooling
  - GitHub Actions for: `go test`, `go vet`, `golangci-lint`, `gofmt` check, and Docker build
  - Dependabot for Go modules + GitHub Actions
  - Makefile targets: `make test`, `make lint`, `make run`, `make docker-build`
- Local dev harness
  - `docker-compose.yml` to run exporter + a lightweight stub source (placeholder) + optional Prometheus
  - `./scripts/dev/generate_synthetic_events.sh` to emit sample audit events to stdout/file (temporary)
- Base config model (initial)
  - `listen_addr`, `log_level`
  - `tenant_id` (dev-only for now; will be replaced by multi-tenant config sync)
  - placeholder `export` section for future destinations (intentionally not wired)

## Not included (intentionally)
- No real event ingestion from Redwood services yet
- No destination adapters (Splunk/Datadog/S3)
- No checkpointing / delivery guarantees
- No filtering engine
- No secret management integration

## Testing
- Unit tests for config parsing + validation
- CI builds container image to ensure Dockerfile stays green
- Manual:
  - `make run` (starts HTTP server)
  - `docker compose up --build` (runs exporter + harness components)

## Follow-ups
- ENG-5132: Control plane export config API
- ENG-5144/5151/5156: Destination adapters
- ENG-5168: Metrics + tracing
- ENG-5189: Threat model items (egress allowlist hooks, secrets handling)

## Notes for reviewers
- Config model is deliberately minimal and will churn; goal is to establish patterns (validation, defaults, env overrides).
- Health endpoints are required for upcoming k8s deployment.

Closes: ENG-5139 (bootstrap portion)

title: Add dashboards + alerts for data residency enforcement (blocked requests, policy eval latency)
preview: Summary: This PR adds a first-pass set of dashboards and alerting rules for Hosted data residency enforcement. The focus is on (1) visibility into enforcement outcomes (allowed vs blocked), (2) early detection of regressions in policy evaluation latency, and (3) detecting cross-region routing attempts that should be prevented once enforcement is enabled. Context: As part of Hosted data residency enforcement rollout (region pinning + deterministic request-time blocking), SRE and Security asked for a consistent set of metrics visualizations and actionable alerts. This pack is intended to be deployed alongside the routing/gateway changes and used during dogfood and progressive rollout. What’s included: Grafana dashboard: “Data Residency Enforcement” with panels: Overview: total requests, allowed/blocked, block-rate (%), and top block reasons (5m/1h). Blocked requests by region + endpoint: breakdown by edge region, model route, and request_type (streaming vs non-streaming). Blocked requests by org/project (top N): uses org_id/project_id labels when present; falls back to org_tier if IDs are intentionally redacted in some environments. Policy evaluation latency: p50/p95/p99 over time from residency policy decision histogram. Routing attempts: cross-region routing attempt counter and fallback suppression counter (to validate that fallback is being blocked unless explicitly allowed). Error contract adoption: panel showing residency_violation responses vs generic 5xx on streaming path (to catch the SUP-28577 class of issues). Alerts: 1) residency.block_rate_high (severity=page): triggers when blocked requests spike above expected baseline after filtering out known test orgs. Default threshold: > 2% block-rate AND > 30 blocked requests/min for 10m, grouped by region. 2) residency.block_rate_high_ticket (severity=ticket): triggers at lower thresholds to create early-warning noise without paging. Default threshold: > 0.5% AND > 10/min for 15m. 3) residency.policy_eval_latency_p95 (severity=page): p95 residency decision latency > 25ms for 10m, grouped by region (catches cache misses, policy service slowness, or routing layer regressions). 4) residency.cross_region_attempts (severity=page): any sustained non-zero cross-region routing attempt rate (> 1/min for 10m) after enforcement is enabled (gated via a deployment label so we don’t page during pre-enforcement dry runs). Example queries (PromQL) used in panels/alerts: Block rate (region): sum(rate(redwood_residency_enforcement_decision_total{decision="blocked"}[5m])) by (region) / clamp_min(sum(rate(redwood_residency_enforcement_decision_total[5m])) by (region), 1) Top block reasons: topk(10, sum(rate(redwood_residency_enforcement_decision_total{decision="blocked"}[15m])) by (block_reason)) Policy eval p95 (region): histogram_quantile(0.95, sum(rate(redwood_residency_policy_eval_duration_seconds_bucket[5m])) by (le, region)) Cross-region attempts: sum(rate(redwood_residency_cross_region_routing_attempt_total[5m])) by (src_region, dst_region) Notes/assumptions: Metric names match the current instrumentation plan in routing/gateway branches: redwood_residency_enforcement_decision_total (labels: decision, block_reason, region, endpoint, request_type, org_tier, org_id?, project_id?, policy_id?) redwood_residency_policy_eval_duration_seconds_bucket redwood_residency_cross_region_routing_attempt_total redwood_residency_fallback_suppressed_total If labels diverge slightly in final implementation, we can follow up with a small patch; this PR keeps queries isolated in jsonnet so updates are straightforward. Files changed (high level): dashboards/residency/enforcement-overview.jsonnet (new) dashboards/residency/enforcement-drilldowns.jsonnet (new) dashboards/residency/enforcement-errors.jsonnet (new) alerts/residency-enforcement.rules.yml (new) mixins/residency/README.md (new runbook links + definitions) dashboards/_index.jsonnet (register new dashboard) alerts/_index.yml (register new rules) Review notes: Sean Gallagher (SRE): Requested separating page vs ticket alerts and adding minimum volume gates to avoid paging during low-traffic regions. Addressed by adding dual alerts and clamp/volume checks; also added a panel for raw blocked count next to percentage. Rafael Mendes (SRE Manager): Asked for explicit links to runbooks and to tag alerts with the rollout phase label so we can disable paging during Phase 2 staging validation. Added runbook URLs and a required label residency_enforcement_enabled="true" to paging alerts where applicable. Logan Wright (Telemetry/audit): Confirmed that org_id/project_id labels are acceptable in internal metrics; requested a guard to allow redaction in some environments. Implemented by building panels that work with org_tier and optional org_id/project_id. Mina Haddad (Routing): Verified cross-region attempt panels align with how routing will emit src/dst labels; requested a panel to show fallback suppressed vs fallback attempted. Added a small comparison panel. Testing/verification: Rendered dashboards via jsonnet build locally; validated against staging Prometheus with synthetic counters from the residency canary job. Confirmed alert rules load successfully and evaluate with expected firing behavior when replaying a spike scenario. CI: lint-jsonnet: pass prometheus-rules-validate: pass dashboard-render-smoke: pass Outcome: Merged (squash) after approvals; safe to deploy with the next observability-pack rollout. Follow-ups: (1) Once gateway error contract is fully rolled out on streaming endpoints, we should tighten the “generic 5xx on streaming” panel and convert it into an alert for regressions (tracked in ENG-4833). (2) Consider adding an SLO panel (block-rate budget) per allowlisted enterprise org during Phase 3 rollout.

title: Add Datadog Logs destination adapter + audit tag/facet mapping
preview: Summary: This PR adds a Datadog Logs destination adapter to the audit-log-exporter and establishes a stable mapping from audit event schema v2 -> Datadog tags/facets. It also introduces rate-limit aware batching behavior (primarily for 429/503) so we degrade gracefully under Datadog intake constraints while preserving at-least-once semantics. Context: As part of the SIEM-ready audit export beta, we support Splunk HEC, S3, and Datadog Logs. Datadog differs from Splunk in that (a) intake is strongly rate-limited, and (b) the best user experience is via consistent tagging/faceting for filtering/dashboards in Datadog. This PR aligns with ENG-5156 and the delivery semantics ADR (at-least-once, downstream dedup via event_id). Design notes (high-level): Adapter: Adds `datadog_logs` destination type with `site`, `intake_path`, and `api_key_secret_ref`. We send batches to `https://http-intake.logs.{site}/api/v2/logs` (default) with JSON payloads. Each audit event becomes one log item with a message, attributes, and tags. Tag/facet mapping: We map audit event fields into a consistent set of Datadog tags for filtering. Primary tags: `redwood.tenant_id`, `redwood.workspace_id`, `redwood.category`, `redwood.action`, `redwood.resource_type`, `redwood.actor_type`, and optionally `redwood.actor_id` (only when allowed by audit policy). We also set `service=redwood-audit-export`, `ddsource=redwood`, and `env=<region|deployment>` (exporter runtime env). We keep the raw payload minimal: we do not include request bodies, prompt text, or free-form metadata; event context is limited to schema v2 allowed fields and pre-existing redactions. Rate-limit aware batching: The adapter maintains a per-destination adaptive batch size. On 429, we back off with jitter and reduce batch size (floor 1). On success, we slowly increase toward the configured max (AIMD-like behavior) to recover throughput without oscillation. We also respect `Retry-After` when present and otherwise default to exponential backoff using the exporter retry policy. Checkpointing/dedup: We do not attempt exactly-once delivery. Each log item includes `event_id` as `redwood.event_id` (tag + attribute) so customers can dedup. We preserve ordering within a partition best-effort but do not guarantee strict order under retries. Security/PII: The adapter redacts sensitive fields in logs and avoids logging payload contents. Exporter logs include only: destination id, batch size, response status, dd request id (when provided), and retry wait. API key is loaded via secret ref; no key material in config logs. Tests: Adds unit tests for tag mapping (category->tag), and integration-ish tests via stubbed intake server covering 200, 429 with Retry-After, and 503 transient errors. Operators: Adds additional metrics for adapter status codes and rate-limit backoff behavior to help SRE tune defaults. Rollout: Feature-flagged behind destination validation at control plane; exporter supports the destination type when present in config sync. Follow-ups: (1) support additional Datadog intake paths for Gov/US3 sites (config already allows override); (2) add customer-facing docs and a Console “Test connection” for Datadog (tracked elsewhere).

title: Routing: enforce residency region pinning + disable cross-region fallback by default
preview:
Summary: Integrates Hosted Data Residency “region pinning” policy evaluation into Smart Routing decisions. Requests are constrained to the policy’s allowed_regions and will no longer fail over cross-region by default. Adds structured decision context to routing logs/spans for traceability and downstream error shaping.

Why: We’ve had customer incidents where routing attempted cross-region fallback when a customer expected EU-only processing (see SUP-28441). This PR ensures routing respects residency constraints deterministically, and makes the decision observable (selected region, policy source, and block reason).

Behavior changes: 1) Policy evaluation: Router calls ResidencyPolicyService (cached) at request admission to resolve the effective residency policy (API key > project > org precedence). 2) Region selection: Candidate regions list is intersected with policy.allowed_regions. If the intersection is empty, the request is blocked at routing with a typed error (ResViolated) including a reason code; the gateway PR will map this to the public error contract. 3) Fallback: Cross-region fallback is disabled by default when a residency policy is present. If policy.allow_cross_region_failover=true (explicit) then fallback may occur but only within policy.allowed_regions. 4) Dedicated alignment: Dedicated routes already run in a customer-selected region; for shared routing codepaths we still apply the same allowed_regions intersection to avoid accidental spillover.

Observability: Adds a structured “residency_decision” object to router logs and tracing span attributes (no customer content). Fields include policy_source (org/project/api_key), policy_id (internal UUID), allowed_regions (internal region IDs), primary_region, selected_region, fallback_attempted, fallback_blocked, decision (allow|block), block_reason, and eval_latency_ms.

Safety/rollout: Feature-gated behind routing.residency_enforcement.enabled (default OFF). Dogfood plan is to enable in staging first (INT-9312) and then progressive production rollout by org allowlist.

Testing: Adds unit tests for policy precedence, candidate region intersection, and fallback behavior. Adds a routing integration test harness with synthetic policy responses and verifies that the selected region is stable across retries and that cross-region fallback is blocked.

Notes: This PR intentionally does NOT implement the public HTTP error payload. That is handled in pr-21533 (gateway). This PR focuses on routing correctness + decision tracing.

title: Add Splunk HEC destination adapter (gzip compression, 429 backoff, per-token circuit breaker)
preview: Summary: Implements a new Splunk HEC destination adapter for SIEM-ready audit exports. The adapter supports gzip-compressed payloads, robust handling of downstream throttling (429) with Retry-After-aware exponential backoff + jitter, and a per-token circuit breaker to avoid hot-looping on bad credentials or persistent throttling. Motivation: Beta customers are primarily Splunk-first and we observed exporter lag spikes when HEC returns sustained 429s; this change improves delivery stability and reduces noisy retry behavior. Scope: Adapter implementation + config schema + basic unit tests + metrics hooks. Not included: Splunk indexer acknowledgment (HEC ACK) support (will track separately), advanced event field remapping beyond the audit schema’s SIEM mapping guidance. Delivery semantics: remains at-least-once; downstream Splunk consumers should dedupe on event_id if needed. Config: adds splunk_hec destination type with endpoint_url, token_ref, index/source/sourcetype (optional), max_batch_events/max_batch_bytes, flush_interval, request_timeout, max_retries, circuit_breaker thresholds and cooldown. Security: token is read via secret ref only; adapter logs redact token and never log full request bodies; destination validation ensures https and blocks loopback/private ranges when egress allowlist is enabled. Observability: adds adapter-level counters for requests, bytes, response codes, retries, breaker_open, and last_success_timestamp; integrates into existing exporter metrics namespace. Test plan: unit tests for batching, gzip encoding, retry/backoff timing (using fake clock), circuit breaker transitions; local manual validation against Splunk HEC sandbox container with artificial 429 injection. Checklist: [x] unit tests added/updated [x] lint/format [x] docs updated for destination config [x] metrics added [ ] e2e harness coverage (follow-up in ENG-5195).

title: Add dead-letter queue + guarded replay CLI for failed export batches
preview: Summary: This PR introduces a dead-letter queue (DLQ) for audit export batches that fail delivery after exhausting retries, and adds an operator-facing replay tool with guardrails. This is a reliability/safety feature for the SIEM-ready audit export beta (Splunk HEC / Datadog Logs / S3) to ensure we never silently drop events while still protecting the platform and customers from accidental replay storms. Context / Motivation: During downstream outages (e.g., sustained 429s from Splunk HEC or 403s from mis-scoped Datadog keys), the exporter currently retries with backoff and then surfaces a per-destination error. Without DLQ persistence we risk either (a) losing batches on process/queue churn or (b) having no safe operational path to replay specific failures once a customer fixes credentials/quotas. ENG-5164 calls for a DLQ + replay tooling with explicit operator controls. Design highlights: (1) DLQ record model: When a batch hits max_delivery_attempts OR we detect a non-retryable adapter error, we persist a DLQ record keyed by (tenant_id, export_config_id, checkpoint_range) and store the batch payload (newline-delimited JSON events) in object storage under a structured prefix. DLQ record includes: dlq_id, created_at, tenant_id, export_config_id, destination_type, reason_code, reason_detail (redacted), first_event_ts, last_event_ts, event_count, byte_size, last_attempt_at, attempt_count, and a payload_ref. (2) At-least-once compatibility: Replays may cause duplicate deliveries. We intentionally do not attempt exactly-once; downstream should dedup using event_id (per ADR on delivery semantics). Replay CLI logs a reminder and requires an explicit --ack-duplicates flag. (3) Operator replay CLI: Adds bin/audit-exporter-replay (Go) to list DLQ items and replay in a controlled fashion. Guardrails include: required --tenant-id and --export-config-id; default is dry-run; interactive confirmation unless --yes is provided; bounded selection via --since/--until and --max-items; token-bucket rate limiting (--max-events-per-minute) to avoid spiking adapters; refuses to replay if export config is disabled OR if exporter feature flag is off for that tenant (unless --override-feature-flag is provided, which is restricted to on-call breakglass role). (4) Safety checks: Replay validates destination configuration hash matches the DLQ record by default (prevents replaying old payloads into a newly re-pointed sink). Can be overridden with --ignore-config-hash-mismatch for incident response, but emits a prominent warning. (5) Redaction: DLQ reason_detail is run through the same redaction helper used by adapter logs to avoid accidentally persisting tokens/keys/PII. Payloads contain audit events only (already subject to audit schema/PII policy); we do not include request/response bodies. (6) Observability: Adds metrics: audit_exporter_dlq_enqueued_total{tenant_id_hash,destination_type,reason_code}, audit_exporter_dlq_backlog_items{destination_type}, audit_exporter_dlq_backlog_events{destination_type}, audit_exporter_dlq_replay_total{result}, and audit_exporter_dlq_replay_latency_seconds. Also adds structured logs for DLQ enqueue and replay runs (with correlation_id). User-visible / Ops workflow: - When an adapter can’t deliver a batch and we exceed retry limits, exporter transitions the batch to DLQ and advances the live pipeline checkpoint to prevent system-wide backpressure. - On-call or Support can instruct customer to fix auth/limits, then run replay for the impacted tenant/config/time window. - Replay is incremental and rate-limited; any failures during replay are re-DLQ’d with a new reason_code (replay_failed_*). Example commands: - List backlog: ./bin/audit-exporter-replay dlq list --tenant-id tnt_123 --export-config-id aex_456 --since 2025-02-09T00:00:00Z --until 2025-02-09T06:00:00Z - Dry-run replay (default): ./bin/audit-exporter-replay dlq replay --tenant-id tnt_123 --export-config-id aex_456 --since 2025-02-09T00:00:00Z --max-items 10 - Execute replay with explicit acknowledgements: ./bin/audit-exporter-replay dlq replay --tenant-id tnt_123 --export-config-id aex_456 --since 2025-02-09T00:00:00Z --max-items 50 --max-events-per-minute 12000 --ack-duplicates --yes Testing: - Unit tests for DLQ persistence, reason_code mapping, and redaction - Integration tests using adapter stubs: force 429/403/non-retryable to verify DLQ enqueue and payload roundtrip - Manual: replayed a synthetic backlog into Splunk stub with rate limiting and verified dedup by event_id Related: - Linear: ENG-5164 - ADR: delivery semantics and idempotency (at-least-once + event_id) - Support context: SUP-18531 (perceived missing events during throttling) Notes / Follow-ups: - Dashboard panels/alerts for DLQ backlog are tracked separately (we add metrics here, panels in observability-pack). - For beta, replay CLI is intended for on-call / SRE / Support escalation only; we are not exposing self-serve replay in Console.
text: Summary: This PR introduces a dead-letter queue (DLQ) for audit export batches that fail delivery after exhausting retries, and adds an operator-facing replay tool with guardrails. This is a reliability/safety feature for the SIEM-ready audit export beta (Splunk HEC / Datadog Logs / S3) to ensure we never silently drop events while still protecting the platform and customers from accidental replay storms. Context / Motivation: During downstream outages (e.g., sustained 429s from Splunk HEC or 403s from mis-scoped Datadog keys), the exporter currently retries with backoff and then surfaces a per-destination error. Without DLQ persistence we risk either (a) losing batches on process/queue churn or (b) having no safe operational path to replay specific failures once a customer fixes credentials/quotas. ENG-5164 calls for a DLQ + replay tooling with explicit operator controls. Design highlights: (1) DLQ record model: When a batch hits max_delivery_attempts OR we detect a non-retryable adapter error, we persist a DLQ record keyed by (tenant_id, export_config_id, checkpoint_range) and store the batch payload (newline-delimited JSON events) in object storage under a structured prefix. DLQ record includes: dlq_id, created_at, tenant_id, export_config_id, destination_type, reason_code, reason_detail (redacted), first_event_ts, last_event_ts, event_count, byte_size, last_attempt_at, attempt_count, and a payload_ref. (2) At-least-once compatibility: Replays may cause duplicate deliveries. We intentionally do not attempt exactly-once; downstream should dedup using event_id (per ADR on delivery semantics). Replay CLI logs a reminder and requires an explicit --ack-duplicates flag. (3) Operator replay CLI: Adds bin/audit-exporter-replay (Go) to list DLQ items and replay in a controlled fashion. Guardrails include: required --tenant-id and --export-config-id; default is dry-run; interactive confirmation unless --yes is provided; bounded selection via --since/--until and --max-items; token-bucket rate limiting (--max-events-per-minute) to avoid spiking adapters; refuses to replay if export config is disabled OR if exporter feature flag is off for that tenant (unless --override-feature-flag is provided, which is restricted to on-call breakglass role). (4) Safety checks: Replay validates destination configuration hash matches the DLQ record by default (prevents replaying old payloads into a newly re-pointed sink). Can be overridden with --ignore-config-hash-mismatch for incident response, but emits a prominent warning. (5) Redaction: DLQ reason_detail is run through the same redaction helper used by adapter logs to avoid accidentally persisting tokens/keys/PII. Payloads contain audit events only (already subject to audit schema/PII policy); we do not include request/response bodies. (6) Observability: Adds metrics: audit_exporter_dlq_enqueued_total{tenant_id_hash,destination_type,reason_code}, audit_exporter_dlq_backlog_items{destination_type}, audit_exporter_dlq_backlog_events{destination_type}, audit_exporter_dlq_replay_total{result}, and audit_exporter_dlq_replay_latency_seconds. Also adds structured logs for DLQ enqueue and replay runs (with correlation_id). User-visible / Ops workflow: - When an adapter can’t deliver a batch and we exceed retry limits, exporter transitions the batch to DLQ and advances the live pipeline checkpoint to prevent system-wide backpressure. - On-call or Support can instruct customer to fix auth/limits, then run replay for the impacted tenant/config/time window. - Replay is incremental and rate-limited; any failures during replay are re-DLQ’d with a new reason_code (replay_failed_*). Example commands: - List backlog: ./bin/audit-exporter-replay dlq list --tenant-id tnt_123 --export-config-id aex_456 --since 2025-02-09T00:00:00Z --until 2025-02-09T06:00:00Z - Dry-run replay (default): ./bin/audit-exporter-replay dlq replay --tenant-id tnt_123 --export-config-id aex_456 --since 2025-02-09T00:00:00Z --max-items 10 - Execute replay with explicit acknowledgements: ./bin/audit-exporter-replay dlq replay --tenant-id tnt_123 --export-config-id aex_456 --since 2025-02-09T00:00:00Z --max-items 50 --max-events-per-minute 12000 --ack-duplicates --yes Testing: - Unit tests for DLQ persistence, reason_code mapping, and redaction - Integration tests using adapter stubs: force 429/403/non-retryable to verify DLQ enqueue and payload roundtrip - Manual: replayed a synthetic backlog into Splunk stub with rate limiting and verified dedup by event_id Related: - Linear: ENG-5164 - ADR: delivery semantics and idempotency (at-least-once + event_id) - Support context: SUP-18531 (perceived missing events during throttling) Notes / Follow-ups: - Dashboard panels/alerts for DLQ backlog are tracked separately (we add metrics here, panels in observability-pack). - For beta, replay CLI is intended for on-call / SRE / Support escalation only; we are not exposing self-serve replay in Console.

title: Support backup-oriented batch export mode (pagination + retention guardrails)
preview:
Context: Private DR / compliance asks require a repeatable, non-interactive way to export audit logs for backup/retention evidence. The existing exporter is optimized for ad-hoc, interactive exports and can be slow/fragile when used in scheduled jobs (offset-based pagination, no checkpointing, limited safeguards around retention boundaries).

This PR adds a backup-oriented batch export mode designed for scheduled backups:
- New CLI subcommand: `audit-log-exporter export batch`
- Cursor-based pagination using the API’s stable `after_id`/`next_cursor` semantics (no offset)
- Checkpointing/resume: writes a checkpoint token to disk periodically; supports `--resume` to continue without duplicating output
- Retention guardrails: validates requested time window against server-reported retention (if available) and fails fast with actionable messaging; explicit override flag required to proceed when outside retention
- Bounded exports: `--max-pages`, `--page-size`, and `--max-records` to prevent runaway jobs
- Deterministic output layout suitable for backup:
  - JSONL lines (one event per line)
  - Rotates files by `--chunk-records` or `--chunk-bytes`
  - Writes a manifest containing record counts, min/max event timestamps, and sha256 checksums
- Backoff + retry tuning for long-running jobs, with explicit handling for rate limits and transient gateway errors

Notes:
- This intentionally does not encrypt output itself; at-rest encryption remains the responsibility of the storage backend (S3 SSE-KMS / GCS CMEK / NFS-at-rest). The docs and examples call this out and link to Private backup storage recommendations.
- The exporter continues to support the existing `export` path unchanged.

Testing:
- Unit tests for pagination correctness (no gaps/dupes), checkpoint/resume behavior, and manifest generation
- Integration-ish test using a stub server to emulate retention responses + cursor pagination

Follow-ups:
- Add Prometheus metrics when we wire this into the Private scheduled backup CronJob path (tracked in ENG-9826 for the broader toolkit metrics/alerts).

title: Emit metrics + tracing for Private backup/restore operations (with error categorization)
preview: Motivation: SRE/on-call and customers need a consistent way to answer “are backups running?” and “why did the last restore drill fail?” across VPC + on-prem Private installs. We already log backup/restore steps in the installer, but logs alone are hard to alert on and don’t support reliable aggregation. This PR adds: (1) structured Prometheus metrics for backup + restore operations (success/failure, duration, bytes, last success timestamps), (2) OpenTelemetry tracing spans with consistent attributes for per-step visibility, and (3) explicit error categorization to drive dashboards/alerts and reduce pager noise. Scope: This instruments the control-plane backup/restore path (installer-driven + control-plane endpoints) and adds common helpers used by both “backup create” and “restore apply”. It does not change backup artifact format or encryption behavior. Metrics (Prometheus): - redwood_private_backup_runs_total{result,storage_backend,component,format_version} (counter) - redwood_private_backup_duration_seconds_bucket{result,storage_backend,component} (histogram) - redwood_private_backup_bytes_total{result,storage_backend,component} (counter) - redwood_private_backup_last_success_timestamp_seconds{storage_backend,component,format_version} (gauge) - redwood_private_restore_runs_total{result,source_backend,target_env,format_version} (counter) - redwood_private_restore_duration_seconds_bucket{result,step} (histogram) - redwood_private_restore_validation_failures_total{check_name,error_category} (counter) - redwood_private_backup_restore_errors_total{operation,step,error_category} (counter; emitted on failure paths only) Tracing (OTel): Adds a top-level span per operation: “private.backup.create” and “private.restore.apply”, with child spans for steps: preflight, quiesce, snapshot, encrypt, upload, verify, bootstrap, migrate, validate. Spans include attributes: deployment_id, cluster_id (if known), namespace, storage_backend, artifact_manifest_version, backup_id/restore_id, step, result, error_category. Error categorization: Introduces a shared enum + mapping helper that classifies common failure types in a stable way for alerting/reporting. Categories include: preflight_failed, quiesce_failed, snapshot_failed, encrypt_failed, upload_failed, download_failed, verify_failed, bootstrap_failed, migrate_failed, validation_failed, compatibility_failed, permission_denied, kms_unavailable, timeout, rate_limited, unknown. Mapping is conservative: if we can’t confidently classify, we emit unknown and preserve the raw error message in logs/traces (not as a metric label) to avoid high-cardinality explosions. RBAC / permissions: No new privileges are required for the control plane, but installer pods/jobs must be allowed to emit metrics to the existing collector in Private installs. This PR reuses existing OTEL exporter wiring and the in-cluster Prometheus scrape config. Testing: - Unit tests for error classification mapping and label sanitization - Integration test in staging that runs a forced failure (bad bucket creds) and asserts metrics increments + spans have error_category - Manual validation: ran backup create / restore apply in dev VPC env; verified dashboard panels populate (see follow-up in observability-pack PR). Notes on cardinality: - No metric labels include backup_id, restore_id, customer name, or raw error strings - deployment_id is included only as a tracing attribute (not a metric label) - component label is constrained to a small set (control_plane_db, control_plane_config, k8s_manifests, audit_logs_export) - step label is constrained to known step names and used only on histogram for restore duration per step (bounded) Follow-ups: - Alert rules + dashboards land in observability-pack (PR-94) - SLO definitions tracked in slo-toolkit (PR-67) - Backup status endpoint is in PR-24318 (separate) which will consume these metrics for UI surfacing

title: Private: expose backup status endpoints from control plane
preview:
Motivation: Private customers (and our own SRE/Solutions workflows) need a consistent way to surface “backup readiness” without scraping CronJob logs or inferring state from storage backends. This PR adds control-plane API endpoints that report backup status and last-success timestamps per tenant/installation, with strict authz and safe redaction (no bucket names, object keys, or secret material). This unblocks Console UI + automated restore drills to gate on “recent successful backup” and to page on backup job regressions.

What’s included: Adds a BackupStatus read model persisted by the backup orchestrator after each run (success/failure), keyed by tenant_id and environment_id; Read-only endpoints under the Private API surface; Includes timestamps (last_success_at, last_attempt_at), status enum, last_error_category + last_error_message (truncated), artifact metadata (format_version, bytes_total), and a computed “staleness” field based on configured schedule; RBAC scopes: tenant:read plus a new private:backup_status:read for installer/service automation; Optional cluster-wide endpoint for platform operators (requires internal operator role); Adds OpenAPI spec + SDK client stub used by Console; Adds audit log events for reads of backup status (low volume) and writes on backup completion.

Endpoints: GET /v1/private/tenants/{tenant_id}/backup/status -> returns latest status record for that tenant; GET /v1/private/backup/status -> operator-only list endpoint with pagination/filtering by status/staleness (intended for internal fleet dashboards; not exposed in customer docs yet).

Non-goals / out of scope: No “trigger backup” endpoint (installer/cronjob remains source of truth); No exposure of artifact locations (bucket/key) or encryption/key IDs; No attempt to infer state by reading object storage; This does not change backup/restore format.

Security + privacy notes: last_error_message is truncated to 512 chars and scrubbed via existing log redaction utility; artifact_metadata excludes backend identifiers; list endpoint is operator-only and excluded from external docs generation.

Testing: Unit tests for status model serialization, authz gates, and redaction; API integration tests covering success and failure flows; Added a contract test asserting the response schema remains backwards compatible (fields only added, never removed) to support Console; Load test sanity: list endpoint paginates and enforces max page size.

Rollout plan: Ship behind feature flag private.backupStatusEndpoints (default on for staging, off for prod) with a follow-up to enable for Private GA once Console is ready; SRE to validate via restore drill automation and dashboard wiring.

Checklist: [x] OpenAPI updated; [x] Authz reviewed (Marcus); [x] Audit logging for reads/writes; [x] Metrics emitted on write path (status_write_success, status_write_latency_ms); [x] Docs note added to internal runbook; [x] Backwards compatible response contract.

Refs: Linear ENG-9826/ENG-9827; related installer work in redwood-private-installer PR-482/PR-489; observability pack PR-94 depends on status fields last_success_at + staleness_minutes.

title: Implement S3 destination adapter (batching, partitioning, checkpoints, retries)
preview:
Context: This PR adds the first destination adapter for the SIEM-ready audit export beta: S3 delivery. The adapter is built around (a) deterministic partitioning for cost/operability, (b) bounded batching for throughput, and (c) at-least-once delivery via checkpoint persistence.

Linear: ENG-5144

What’s included:
- New S3 destination adapter implementing the exporter Destination interface
- Batching policy: flush on max_events, max_bytes, or flush_interval (defaults tuned for beta)
- Partitioning scheme (configurable prefix + time partitioning + tenant scoping) to avoid hot prefixes and keep search-friendly layouts
- Checkpoint persistence for at-least-once semantics (write object -> persist checkpoint). Cursor is (emitted_at, event_id) and is treated as an opaque ordering token by downstream
- Retry/backoff for S3 throttling/transient errors (SlowDown/503/timeouts) with jitter and a max retry cap; non-retryable errors go to DLQ (DLQ plumbing is stubbed behind an interface; full operator tooling comes in ENG-5164)
- Minimal metrics around adapter throughput, batch size, upload latency, and retry counts
- Unit tests for batching edge cases (size boundaries, timer flush, retry classification) and a small local dev harness doc

Out of scope (follow-ups):
- SSE-KMS + cross-account assume role (tracked in ENG-5148; implemented in PR #1068)
- Integration tests with real AWS / localstack matrix (tracked in ENG-5195)
- Console UI + config CRUD (control-plane work)

Implementation details:
- Object format: newline-delimited JSON (NDJSON) where each line is a single canonical audit event payload (already emitted by upstream as JSON). We intentionally avoid wrapping in a top-level array to support streaming/partial reads in customer tooling.
- Object key layout (default): s3://<bucket>/<prefix>/tenant_id=<tenant>/workspace_id=<workspace>/dt=YYYY-MM-DD/hour=HH/<destination_id>_<unix_ms>_<batch_seq>.ndjson
  - tenant_id/workspace_id are included to make customer-side permissions/audits easier when exporting multiple workspaces into a shared bucket.
  - dt/hour partitions keep file counts reasonable for typical SIEM collectors.
- Checkpoint store: uses the exporter’s checkpoint repository abstraction backed by the existing service DB (per-tenant scoped). Each destination has a single monotonically advancing cursor.
  - We only advance the checkpoint after the upload succeeds.
  - If checkpoint update fails after upload, we will re-upload on restart (duplicates are expected; downstream should dedup on event_id).
- Retry policy: exponential backoff with decorrelated jitter. We treat 429/SlowDown/5xx/timeouts as retryable. 403/AccessDenied and 400/BadRequest are non-retryable and are surfaced as destination health errors.
- Guardrails: adapter enforces a max object size (hard cap) and will split batches accordingly; also validates bucket + prefix format early so we fail fast during config validation.

Operational notes:
- Adapter emits structured logs with destination_id/tenant_id/workspace_id, but never logs event payloads.
- Added a per-destination circuit-breaker-ish counter (consecutive failures) used by the exporter loop to mark destination unhealthy (full auto-pausing is in PR #1119).

Testing:
- Unit tests for batching and retry classification (go test ./...)
- Manual smoke test using AWS dev bucket documented in docs/dev/s3-smoke-test.md

Rollout:
- No runtime behavior change unless S3 destination type is configured. Feature flag gating is handled upstream in control-plane; this PR is safe to deploy to exporter environments ahead of time.

title: Normalize region identifiers in metrics pipeline (canonical mapping + normalized_region tag)
preview:
Context: The Unit Economics dashboard (ENG-8426) requires stable grouping by region, but today we emit a mix of cloud region strings (e.g., "us-east-1"), internal colo labels (e.g., "iad-1"), and provider-specific variants (e.g., "eu-west" vs "eu-west-1"). This causes dropped rows in aggregation (joins fail), duplicate region groups in dashboards, and inconsistent reconciliation vs Finance rate cards.

This PR introduces a canonical region mapping and updates ingestion so analytics receives a single, normalized region dimension.

What’s included: (1) Canonical region mapping table + versioned source-of-truth (2) Ingestion changes to emit `normalized_region` (and preserve raw `region` for debugging) (3) Guardrails for unknown/unmapped regions (4) Backfill-friendly behavior and unit tests.

Design notes: - The mapping is intentionally small and stable; we do NOT map down to per-AZ. - We keep `region_raw`/`region_source` so oncall can debug mapping mistakes without digging through gateway logs. - Unknown mappings are emitted as `unknown` and counted (metric + log) instead of dropping events, to avoid silent undercounting.

Implementation details: - Added `region_canonical_map` (warehouse + service config) keyed by (provider, region_raw) -> normalized_region. - Updated metrics pipeline ingestion (gateway + runtime collector) to call `normalizeRegion(provider, regionRaw)` and attach `normalized_region` tag on all token metering events. - Added a daily diff check in CI to detect accidental drift in the mapping table (mostly for typos / duplicate keys).

Example mapping entry (illustrative): provider=aws, region_raw=eu-west -> normalized_region=aws/eu-west-1; provider=internal, region_raw=iad-1 -> normalized_region=internal/us-east.

Rollout plan: - Deploy behind feature flag `metrics.normalized_region.enabled` default off. - Enable for 5% of hosted traffic in us-east + eu-west, validate that (a) event volume stays flat, (b) unknown_region_count < 0.1%, (c) unit econ aggregation join coverage improves. - Ramp to 100% hosted, then dedicated/private collectors.

Validation: - Compared pre/post event counts by hour: within ±0.2%. - Verified no increase in tag cardinality (normalized values are constrained).

Follow-ups: - Once roll-out completes, update unit economics views to prefer `normalized_region` and only fall back to raw for legacy windows (separate PR in ENG-8426 branch).

Context: The Unit Economics dashboard (ENG-8426) requires stable grouping by region, but today we emit a mix of cloud region strings (e.g., "us-east-1"), internal colo labels (e.g., "iad-1"), and provider-specific variants (e.g., "eu-west" vs "eu-west-1"). This causes dropped rows in aggregation (joins fail), duplicate region groups in dashboards, and inconsistent reconciliation vs Finance rate cards.

This PR introduces a canonical region mapping and updates ingestion so analytics receives a single, normalized region dimension.

What’s included: (1) Canonical region mapping table + versioned source-of-truth (2) Ingestion changes to emit `normalized_region` (and preserve raw `region` for debugging) (3) Guardrails for unknown/unmapped regions (4) Backfill-friendly behavior and unit tests.

Design notes: - The mapping is intentionally small and stable; we do NOT map down to per-AZ. - We keep `region_raw`/`region_source` so oncall can debug mapping mistakes without digging through gateway logs. - Unknown mappings are emitted as `unknown` and counted (metric + log) instead of dropping events, to avoid silent undercounting.

Implementation details: - Added `region_canonical_map` (warehouse + service config) keyed by (provider, region_raw) -> normalized_region. - Updated metrics pipeline ingestion (gateway + runtime collector) to call `normalizeRegion(provider, regionRaw)` and attach `normalized_region` tag on all token metering events. - Added a daily diff check in CI to detect accidental drift in the mapping table (mostly for typos / duplicate keys).

Example mapping entry (illustrative): provider=aws, region_raw=eu-west -> normalized_region=aws/eu-west-1; provider=internal, region_raw=iad-1 -> normalized_region=internal/us-east.

Rollout plan: - Deploy behind feature flag `metrics.normalized_region.enabled` default off. - Enable for 5% of hosted traffic in us-east + eu-west, validate that (a) event volume stays flat, (b) unknown_region_count < 0.1%, (c) unit econ aggregation join coverage improves. - Ramp to 100% hosted, then dedicated/private collectors.

Validation: - Compared pre/post event counts by hour: within ±0.2%. - Verified no increase in tag cardinality (normalized values are constrained).

Follow-ups: - Once roll-out completes, update unit economics views to prefer `normalized_region` and only fall back to raw for legacy windows (separate PR in ENG-8426 branch).
